role_summary: Novelist and essayist; chronicler of the civil rights movement in essays including The Fire Next Time (1963) and Notes of a Native Son (1955); commentator on the movement from a writer's vantage rather than an organizer's.

photo.alt: James Baldwin, photographed in Hyde Park, London, 1969

ai_reading: Five corpus voices discuss Baldwin: William Lamar Strickland (entry 127, Northern Black Power), Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91, Black children's-literature author), Ruby Sales (entry 112, SpiritHouse Project), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), and Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34, SNCC Washington / Carmichael memoir editor). The cluster is heavy on writers (Walter, Thelwell) and the figures whose own oral histories carry strong literary-or-theological framings (Sales, Vivian). The embedding therefore reads Baldwin as discussed primarily in literary-theological registers rather than in field-organizing context, consistent with his cultural placement as the movement's most-cited essayist rather than its strategist.
biographical_paragraph: James Baldwin is discussed in this corpus by 5 interviewees across the catalog (William Lamar Strickland (entry 127), Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91), Ruby Sales (entry 112), C. T. Vivian (entry 134), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats James Baldwin as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

sources.title: David Leeming, James Baldwin: A Biography
